role_summary: Pastor of Mount Zion Baptist Church in Seattle (1958-1998); Morehouse College classmate of Martin Luther King Jr.; organizer of the Central Area Civil Rights Committee and co-founder of Liberty Bank and the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center.
ai_reading: The embedding finds Samuel Berry McKinney's testimony closest to two figures from outside his own region and vocation. By the precomputed semantic-neighbor tally in this corpus, his strongest neighbor is Amos C. Brown (entry 2, 122 shared retrievals), the San Francisco NAACP figure and Third Baptist pastor, a pairing of two West Coast Black Baptist ministers that reads as expected; but the second strongest is Robert L. Carter (entry 108, 116 retrievals), the NAACP Legal Defense Fund attorney who argued school-desegregation cases, with Charles McLaurin (entry 17, 103) and C. T. Vivian (entry 134, 98) close behind. The cosine space groups McKinney's church-and-community-development account next to a constitutional-litigation career more readily than next to most of the pastors he names in his own interview. On the Nonviolence as Theology versus Armed Self-Defense axis his normalized position is +0.84, sixth of the 136 audited interviews toward the Nonviolence as Theology pole, which lines up with the cultural framing of a King classmate and lifelong Baptist preacher; the raw spread on this axis is narrow, so the reading is a relative ranking rather than a strong absolute lean, but it places him in the same tail as Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132) and Joseph Echols Lowery (entry 66), the recurring pattern in this catalog where SCLC-network clergy register toward the nonviolence pole their public record carries.
biographical_paragraph: Samuel Berry McKinney was born on December 28, 1926, in Flint, Michigan, the son of a Baptist minister, and the family moved to Cleveland, Ohio, when he was an infant after his father was called to Antioch Baptist Church there [src: 2, 4]. He entered Morehouse College in 1944, was drafted into the segregated Army Air Forces during World War II, and returned to finish his degree on the G.I. Bill; a required Bible course under George Kelsey and the example of Morehouse president Benjamin Mays turned him from his intended path in law toward the ministry [src: 2, 3]. One of his freshman classmates was Martin Luther King Jr., then fifteen [src: 2, 4]. After seminary at Colgate Rochester Divinity School and a first pastorate at Olney Street Baptist Church in Providence, Rhode Island, McKinney moved to Seattle in February 1958 to lead Mount Zion Baptist Church, where he remained as pastor until 1998 and again from 2005 to 2008 [src: 2, 3]. In 1961 he invited King to preach in Seattle; the host venue, First Presbyterian Church, withdrew its agreement shortly before the event, and McKinney relocated the appearance rather than accept the church's offer to cover his advertising costs [src: 2, 3]. He helped organize the Central Area Civil Rights Committee in 1963 to coordinate local desegregation campaigns, co-founded Liberty Bank, the first Black-owned bank in the Pacific Northwest, and in 1966 co-founded and served as first president of the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center [src: 2, 3, 4]. McKinney died on April 7, 2018, at the age of 91; in 2014 the city of Seattle renamed the blocks of 19th Avenue beside Mount Zion as Rev. Dr. S. McKinney Avenue [src: 3, 5]. His oral history was recorded in Seattle on April 17, 2013, by David Cline and is held at the Library of Congress as entry 115 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

photo.alt: Reverend Doctor Samuel B. McKinney at the February 2014 Seattle ceremony naming a section of 19th Avenue in his recognition.
photo.caption: Samuel Berry McKinney at the February 10, 2014 City of Seattle ceremony renaming the blocks of 19th Avenue beside Mount Zion Baptist Church as Rev. Dr. S. McKinney Avenue.

interview_snippets.speaker: Samuel Berry McKinney
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling his Morehouse freshman class in 1944, when World War II had thinned the rolls and pulled enrollment ages down:
interview_snippets.quote: Squirt, named Martin Luther King Jr. was in the same freshman class. He was 15.

interview_snippets.speaker: Samuel Berry McKinney
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the young King as a Morehouse student, a detail from McKinney's first-person memory of their shared campus:
interview_snippets.quote: It's interesting to note that to my knowledge, he took part in every oratorical contest and never won.

interview_snippets.speaker: Samuel Berry McKinney
interview_snippets.lead_in: On a lunch stop during basic training in the segregated Army Air Forces, where his unit ate in a colored dining room and looked across at the white one:
interview_snippets.quote: There were tablecloths, then in the silverware and German prisoners of war. Sitting down to the table eating, we had on the United States Army uniform. And these were supposed to be enemies of our country, because they were white.

interview_snippets.speaker: Samuel Berry McKinney
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the argument from his Morehouse Bible teacher George Kelsey that turned him from law toward the ministry:
interview_snippets.quote: He said, the law functions on the back end, religion functions on the front end. And as one thinks so, one is, thought proceeds action.

interview_snippets.speaker: Samuel Berry McKinney
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the difference he found between southern and northern segregation after moving from Cleveland and the South to Seattle:
interview_snippets.quote: Someone said at that time the difference between the South and the North, the South didn't care how close blacks and whites got so wrong, we didn't get too high. The North didn't care how high we got provided we didn't get too close.

interview_snippets.speaker: Samuel Berry McKinney
interview_snippets.lead_in: Summing up the church-centered economic-development work alongside the protests, the credit union, the bank, the housing, and the training center:
interview_snippets.quote: So I have been involved in doing what I feel is a lot of good around the city and community. Not only protesting, but also building.
